Summary: This study developed and validated an integrated model incorporating deep learning (DL), hand-crafted radiomics, and clinical and US features for diagnosing central lymph node metastasis (CLNM) in patients with papillary thyroid cancer (PTC). The integrated model outperformed individual DL and hand-crafted radiomics models, as well as junior and senior radiologists, in reducing missed CLNM and unnecessary central lymph node dissection (CLND). The study also used SHAP and Grad-CAM for model explanation, enhancing the acceptance of AI-assisted US diagnosis.

Item1.reason: The authors state that "The checklist for Artificial Intelligence in Medical Imaging (CLAIM) and EvaluAtion of Radiomics research (CLEAR) were applied as step-by-step reporting guideline for this study, which is presented in a Supplementary Material 1 and 2"

Item2.reason: The authors define inclusion and exclusion criteria for patient selection.

Item3.reason: The reference standard is histopathology, which is a high-quality reference standard.

Item4.reason: The study includes data from two institutions.

Item5.reason: The study uses ultrasound (US) imaging, which is a common and standardized imaging technique for thyroid cancer assessment. The US features are based on C-TIRADS and ATA guidelines.

Item6.reason: The authors mention the US machines used and that the data was stored in DICOM format. They also describe the US features based on C-TIRADS and ATA guidelines.

Item7.reason: The thyroid US examination was performed within one month before the operation, which is an appropriate interval to validate the presence or absence of target conditions.

Condition1.reason: The study includes manual segmentation of the primary lesion images to develop the deep learning and hand-crafted radiomics models.

Condition2.reason: The segmentation was performed manually by radiologists.

Item8.reason: The authors describe the manual segmentation process using ITK-SNAP and the consensus reached by two radiologists. They also mention the consistency analysis with a Dice coefficient of 0.946.

Item9.reason: The segmentation was performed manually, not fully automatically.

Item10.reason: The ROIs were reviewed by a radiologist with seven years of experience reaching a consensus. This reflects clinical practice.

Condition3.reason: The study includes the development of a hand-crafted radiomics model.

Item11.reason: The authors describe the pre-processing steps for the deep learning model, including resizing and normalization. For the radiomics model, they mention the extraction of wavelet decompositions and Laplacian of Gaussian features.

Item12.reason: The authors used pyradiomics (version 3.1.0) for feature extraction.

Item13.reason: The authors describe the feature types (first-order statistics, texture features, shape features, wavelet decompositions, and Laplacian of Gaussian features) and provide a supplementary material listing the definitions of each feature group. They also describe the CNN architecture and image augmentation techniques.

Condition4.reason: The study includes tabular data in the form of clinical and US features, as well as the extracted radiomic features.

Condition5.reason: The study includes the development of a deep learning model using a CNN.

Item14.reason: The authors used LASSO logistic regression to select radiomics features, which can be considered a method for removing non-robust features.

Item15.reason: The authors used LASSO logistic regression to select the most informative features.

Item16.reason: The authors performed multivariate analysis to select clinical and US features, and used LASSO for feature selection in radiomics. This suggests consideration of the appropriateness of dimensionality compared to data size.

Item17.reason: The authors do not explicitly mention a robustness assessment of the end-to-end deep learning pipeline in a test-retest setting or with image perturbations.

Item18.reason: The authors state that "The participants were randomly divided into training and independent test cohorts for further analysis."

Item19.reason: The authors performed multivariate analysis to identify independent risk factors for CLNM, which can be considered an attempt to handle confounding factors.

Item20.reason: The authors report AUC, accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, PPV, and NPV for evaluating the performance of the models. They also include ROC curves.

Item21.reason: The authors do not include uncertainty measures such as confidence intervals or standard deviations.

Item22.reason: The authors do not mention calibration assessment of the final model.

Item23.reason: The study does not compare the performance of the integrated model with uni-parametric imaging or provide justification for the use of multi-parametric imaging.

Item24.reason: The study compares the performance of the integrated model with junior and senior radiologists, which can be considered a non-radiomic approach representative of clinical practice. They also use DeLong's test to compare the AUCs.

Item25.reason: The study does not include a comparison with a simple baseline reference model.

Item26.reason: The authors state that "The participants were randomly divided into training and independent test cohorts for further analysis."

Item27.reason: The study includes data from two institutions, and the model is tested on an independent dataset.

Item28.reason: The authors state that "Other analyzed datasets from the current study are not publicly available because the data contain information that may compromise patients"

Item29.reason: The authors state that "The codes used during the current study can be accessed at https://github.com/yytangxiaoyuan/metastasis_predict_model/tree/main."

Item30.reason: The authors do not explicitly state that the final model is shared in the form of a raw model file or as a ready-to-use system.
